topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in Dr. Albright’s office felt thick, almost viscous, clinging to my skin like damp wool. I kept drumming my fingers against the worn wooden desk, the rhythmic tapping a desperate attempt to keep my hands from shaking. Every time I glanced at the calendar pinned above his blotter, the date seemed to stretch into an endless, terrifying expanse. He hadn't said much, just a curt nod during our last meeting, a finality in his eyes that settled deep in my stomach. I reread the final chapter outline, the carefully constructed scaffolding of my entire academic life, and felt a sudden, nauseating lurch. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding deafening in the sudden silence. I needed air, needed distance, but the hallway felt too exposed, too much like a pressure cooker about to blow.

[story 2]

I spent the entire afternoon meticulously re-alphabetizing my notes, a task utterly pointless but necessary. My gaze kept drifting to the door, a solid barrier separating me from the impending shift. My jaw was tight, a hard knot of tension pulling at my molars. When Dr. Albright finally emerged, his usual composed demeanor seemed strained, his movements slightly too quick. "Just wanted to confirm the final revisions," he murmured, his voice lacking its customary warmth. I managed a tight smile, forcing my expression into something neutral, though my palms were slick against the laminated cover of my research binder. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the action feel monumental. I wanted to ask him something—anything—but the words caught, lodged behind a sudden, paralyzing fear of saying the wrong thing.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights in the library seemed to buzz with an aggressive intensity, mirroring the frantic thumping beneath my ribs. I kept checking my phone, though no messages had arrived; I just stared at the screen, waiting for a signal that wouldn't come. Every time I heard footsteps approaching my study carrel, I would freeze, my breath catching in shallow, quick bursts. When the door swung open, it was him. He looked tired, a shadow of his usual sharp focus lingering around his eyes. "I'm finalizing my departure plans," he stated, the words flat, devoid of the usual academic preamble. A cold wave washed over me, making the small hairs on my arms stand up. I gripped my pen so tightly the plastic threatened to crack, my entire body coiled, ready to bolt, yet rooted firmly to the chair.
